intro: The AI arms race is heating up on multiple fronts today. Meta is making bold claims that its in-training model "Watermelon" has caught up to OpenAI's GPT-5.5, while a Guardian investigation reveals that Britain's ambitious AI growth zones may be built on broken promises — with a landmark Scottish datacentre project exposed for having no realistic path to meeting its renewable energy commitments. We've also got stories on Israel's AI-powered targeting system identifying 850,000 targets across Gaza and Lebanon, China's push to crack robotic dexterity, wealthy parents handing their kids' education over to AI tutors, and the ongoing fight over copyright in the age of generative AI.

stories.title: Meta sizes up GPT-5.5 with 'Watermelon'
stories.summary: Meta's superintelligence chief Alexandr Wang reportedly told employees that their model currently in training, codenamed 'Watermelon,' has matched OpenAI's GPT-5.5 in performance. Watermelon uses roughly 10x the compute of its predecessor Muse Spark, which launched in April 2025. Wang also teased an upcoming Opus-level coding model and a Muse Spark update with significant coding and agentic improvements. However, CEO Mark Zuckerberg noted that industry-wide agent progress hasn't accelerated as expected. The competitive landscape remains challenging, as other frontier models continue advancing rapidly.
stories.opinion: This article reads as a fairly standard AI industry hype piece, reporting internal claims from Meta leadership without independent benchmarks or verification. The claim that Watermelon matches GPT-5.5 comes from an internal town hall, not from published evaluations, so it should be taken with significant skepticism. The article itself acknowledges the key problem: even if Watermelon reaches GPT-5.5 parity, the frontier keeps moving, with competitors already pushing beyond that level. Zuckerberg…

stories.title: Israeli command system identified 850,000 targets in Gaza and Lebanon wars, says supplier
stories.summary: Elbit Systems, Israel's largest arms supplier, revealed in a presentation that the Israeli Tzayad digital army command and control system identified approximately 850,000 targets in real time during the wars in Gaza and Lebanon. The system mapped people, vehicles, and other objects, detecting roughly 1,000 potential targets per day over the first two years of conflict starting from October 7th. The Tzayad programme operated across all of the Israeli military's theatres of war.
stories.opinion: This report is deeply alarming and raises profound ethical and legal concerns. The sheer scale of 850,000 identified 'targets' — averaging 1,000 per day — in densely populated areas like Gaza is staggering and raises serious questions about how broadly 'target' is being defined. The fact that an arms company is essentially boasting about this capability in a corporate presentation is disturbing, as it frames mass surveillance and targeting of a civilian population as a product feature rather th…

stories.title: ‘It’s smoke and mirrors’: hope turns to fear in Scottish village chosen for AI datacentre
stories.summary: A Scottish village in Lanarkshire, chosen as the site for a large AI datacentre, is experiencing growing disillusionment as initial promises of economic transformation and future jobs give way to fears among residents. Local people suspect they have been misled about the benefits of the development, with some fearing they may have to sell their properties. Related reporting suggests the project has no realistic prospect of meeting its renewable energy commitments, raising broader questions about the feasibility of Britain's AI growth zone plans.
stories.opinion: This story highlights a recurring pattern in large-scale tech infrastructure projects: grand promises of economic revitalization are made to gain community buy-in, but the reality often falls short, leaving local residents bearing the costs. The characterization as 'smoke and mirrors' suggests a troubling lack of transparency from developers and possibly from government officials who championed the project. AI datacentres are enormous energy consumers, and the revelation that renewables promise…

stories.title: Revealed: landmark Scottish AI project has no prospect of meeting renewables promise
stories.summary: A Guardian investigation has revealed that a major AI datacentre project planned for Lanarkshire, Scotland, has misrepresented its renewable energy plans. The project, which promised to bring jobs and prosperity to the rural area, claimed it would channel the equivalent of a nuclear reactor's worth of power to the site using renewables. However, government officials and developers have privately acknowledged there is a significant 'issue' with power provision, and the project has no realistic prospect of meeting its renewable energy commitments. The investigation suggests the renewable energy promises were described as 'smoke and mirrors,' turning local hope into fear in the Scottish village chosen for the development.
stories.opinion: This report highlights a deeply troubling pattern in the AI infrastructure boom: companies and governments making grandiose promises about green energy to win public support for massive datacentre projects, while privately knowing those commitments are unachievable. The energy demands of AI are enormous and growing rapidly, and it is dishonest to dress up these developments as environmentally sustainable when the renewable infrastructure simply doesn't exist to support them. Rural communities d…

stories.title: What are Britain’s AI growth zones and are the plans feasible or ‘complete bunk’?
stories.summary: The Guardian investigated Britain's AI growth zones, including a landmark Scottish AI datacentre project in Lanarkshire intended to run on renewables and create thousands of jobs. The investigation found that the project has no prospect of meeting its renewables promise by 2030. Sources described the government's plans as at best unclear and at worst 'complete bunk' and 'smoke and mirrors,' with local hope turning to fear in the Scottish village chosen for the AI datacentre.
stories.opinion: The article appears to be a well-sourced investigative piece that critically examines the feasibility of the UK government's AI infrastructure ambitions. The use of strong language from sources like 'complete bunk' and 'smoke and mirrors' suggests significant skepticism about whether these plans are realistic or primarily political announcements. The focus on the gap between renewable energy promises and practical reality is a legitimate and important line of inquiry, as governments worldwide o…

stories.title: I don't want a US tech bro as a patron - which is why artists must defend our copyright in the age of AI | Anna Funder
stories.summary: Anna Funder argues that Australian artists and creators must defend their copyright against US tech companies seeking to use their creative works for free or cheaply to train AI systems. She recounts visiting Canberra with fellow creators to advocate for stronger copyright protections, emphasizing that creative works are the property of their makers and should not be surrendered to foreign corporations seeking to profit from AI without fair compensation.
stories.opinion: I find Funder's argument compelling and important. The framing of AI training on copyrighted works as a form of digital colonialism—where powerful US tech companies extract creative value from other countries without compensation—is apt. Copyright exists precisely to ensure creators can sustain themselves, and the 'move fast and break things' ethos of Silicon Valley shouldn't override those rights. However, the debate is nuanced: there are legitimate questions about fair use, transformative wor…

stories.title: China wants to solve the hardest problem in robotics – making hands
stories.summary: China is intensely focused on developing dextrous robotic hands, considered one of the hardest challenges in robotics, as a key step toward making humanoid robots practical and useful rather than mere gimmicks. The article highlights the extraordinary complexity of human hands and the intricate neurological processes behind everyday tasks like tying shoelaces or buttoning shirts. This effort is part of a broader race to develop 'embodied AI' — robots that can physically interact with the world in sophisticated ways. China appears to be making this a strategic priority in its robotics and AI development programs.
stories.opinion: This is a genuinely important area of technological development, and China's focus on it is strategically astute. Dextrous manipulation has long been the bottleneck preventing humanoid robots from being truly useful in real-world settings — locomotion and perception have advanced significantly, but without capable hands, robots remain limited. The fact that China is prioritizing this suggests a serious long-term vision for deploying humanoid robots in manufacturing, elder care, and other labor-…

stories.title: Some of the nation’s rich are letting AI teach their kids
stories.summary: Wealthy American families are paying tens of thousands of dollars to enroll their children in AI-driven schools like Forge Prep and Alpha School, where AI tutors and interactive workshops replace traditional education. Silicon Valley parents are major adopters, with one venture capitalist planning to spend $75,000 on an AI-based kindergarten, arguing traditional education is 'broken.' The article raises concerns about using sycophantic AI to teach critical thinking, and notes that Alpha School's cofounder plans to exclude 'hot-button social issues' from the curriculum, which could mean omitting topics like women's rights, slavery, and immigration history.
stories.opinion: This is a deeply troubling trend that essentially turns children of the wealthy into beta testers for unproven educational technology. The irony is thick: parents who can afford any educational option are choosing to gamble on AI systems known for hallucination and sycophancy to teach their children critical thinking. The venture capitalist's dismissal of traditional pedagogy as mere 'recitation of facts' reveals a profound ignorance of how modern education actually works. More concerning is th…

tweet_headlines.author: Granite0x
tweet_headlines.headline: Addy Osmani, Google's Gemini engineering lead, open-sourced his personal Claude setup — __68,925 stars__ and counting.
tweet_headlines.source_text:
He leads engineering on Gemini at Google.

instead of keeping his Claude setup private, he open-sourced it.

Addy Osmani. That Google: Chrome DevTools lead, "Learning JavaScript Design Patterns" author.

'agent-skills' - his personal loadout. Drop-in for any project.

68,925 https://t.co/XsRslbCScc https://t.co/At82Ae2JH4

tweet_headlines.author: dzhng
tweet_headlines.headline: David Zhang built a Fable skill that __scans your codebase and interviews you__ to close known unknowns — then sweeps for the ones you never thought of.
tweet_headlines.source_text:
Fed this article to Fable and we created an explore-unknowns skill

It scans your codebase, then interviews you one question at a time to close the known unknowns

Then sweeps for the unknown unknowns you never thought of

Now part of my software factory: https://t.co/WcLfM9NhP6 https://t.co/wJzW8oEyH5
